@eric-haibin-lin @KellenSunderland The reason this is not being cached the way `log_verbose_` is, is mostly because users can change the value of the env var at runtime more than once. An example of such a situation can be found [here](https://github.com/mkolod/incubator-mxnet/blob/tensorrt_integration/tests/python/tensorrt/test_tensorrt_lenet5.py#L77::L85) in the unit tests. The reason for this being an env var was to not break any of the C APIs that are tied to all language frontends. Allowing the check at simple_bind time each time allows to run with or without TensorRT, to compare performance, for instance. Note that this env var only gets checked twice in two different methods during bind time, while `log_verbose_` gets checked 9 times. Note that once a symbol is bound, the env var no longer gets checked. It's not common for a symbol to be rebound, and even when that happens, it's just during setup, not anwhere else later on.

I'm not sure, according to the RFC:
```
The "." and subsequent digits ("sss") are optional.  However the "."
MUST NOT appear unless at least one following digit also appears.
```
So testing if a `.` is present seems to be the best option here. If a `.` is present it MUST be parsable with `%Y%m%d%H%M%S.%f`. If it isn't parsable, then it also won't be parsable with `%Y%m%d%H%M%S` because there is a `.` in the `time_val` string

One possible case is when you have overloaded constructors when you have mixed placement new and init factory constructors, such as:

```
cl.def("__init__", [](Class &self, double i) { new (&self) Class(i); });
cl.def("__init__", py::init([](int j) { return new Class(j); });
```
when called from Python as `Class(3)` to go to the second overload.  Because the lazy allocation happens during argument loading, the `&self` will get preallocated even though the overload fails, and the other init factories need to deal with it.

I suppose we could deprecated placement new constructors and get rid of this eventually (i.e. in an eventual 3.0), in which case we wouldn't need to worry about this case.

```python
# allennlp/commons/util.py
def get_frozen_and_tunable_parameter_names(model: torch.nn.Module) -> List:
    frozen_parameter_names = []
    tunable_parameter_names = []
    for name, parameter in model.named_parameters():
        if not parameter.requires_grad:
            frozen_parameter_names.append(name)
        else:
            tunable_parameter_names.append(name)
    return [frozen_parameter_names, tunable_parameter_names]
```

1. if deleting a key group results in a quorum that can no longer be satisfied, refuse the change and display an error
2. require users to update the quorum at the same time as deleting a key group for the operation to succeed: `sops groups --delete group1 --update-quorum 2`, or something along those lines

Yes. For now, requests are handled one at a time, one per connection thread. In reality, this doesn't have to be the case. While a single `KmipEngine` will be shared across threads, synchronization only really has to protect data store operations (e.g., data creation, data persistence, data management). Multiple operations could be in execution simultaneously under this model. Some of the state-based features (e.g., the ID placeholder, the protocol version per request) could be persisted to the data store as well.

The reactor pattern could certainly work here. There are many options. I went with the thread-per-connection approach because it was the solution I knew I could get working fastest, since I've written this type of system before. To manage overhead, I'm capping the number of simultaneous connections to 5 (which is the recommended cap for `socket.listen`).

OK, lets define the problem :)
Having
```
s1 = Summary("s1",...)
s2 = Summary("s2",...)
```

The original issue is that:
```
@s1.time()
def f(): pass
```
Produces wrong timings when `f()` is called simultaneously from different threads. My last code solves inter thread issue using TLS, and uses `id(self)` as TSL key to support multiple timer instances within a thread. Such that both `f()` and `g()` (below) work
```
@s1.time()
def g():
    s2.time()(lambda : time.sleep(1))()
```
And both `f()` and `g()` cases are covered with tests.

Now, what do you refer as reentrancy? This? -
```
@s1.time()
def h():
    @s1.time()
    def i(): pass
    i()
```
This will work as well, since each call to `time()` will produce different instances of the `_Time()` with distinct `id(self)`.

P.S. I actually think that the better and simpler idea is to rework both decorator and context manager to use function closure to record the start time. It will be also a bit faster IMHO. I need to think whether its possible to implement it without changing the `time()` semantics.

> If I understand you correctly, you are claiming that the high-frequency scrape case will naturally result in scraping a whitelisted set of metrics.

Yes. Given the data volumes involved with such systems no other way is practical, and I've never seen any other approach taken. Usually you'd be whitelisting on the order of 10 metrics, and with a blacklisting approach the organic growth of metrics would be sufficient to take out such a system.

Unfortunately 3.5 blows up if you even have syntax like this in your *file*, it doesn't matter whether you try executing it or not. So the only way I know to write a test like this is to use `exec`:
```python
code = textwrap.dedent("""
    @acontextmanager
    async def test_issue29692_2():
        ...
""")
ns = {"acontextmanager": acontextmanager}
exec(code, ns)
test_issue29692_2 = ns["test_issue29692_2"]
```
(and then you still need a version check to make sure you only do this on 3.6+, or alternatively try it always and then catch the `SyntaxError` if it doesn't work)

It might make sense to consolidate with the previous test... like make a version dependent list of test context managers, and then do a `for` loop over it? Or not, sometimes it's simpler just to have a bit of copy-paste in test code :-)

Longer explanation: `threading.Lock()` creates a single, discrete lock. To illustrate, imagine a world where we have two separate locks that lock two separate things:

To illustrate, consider a kitchen with two cooks. Both cooks want to make scrambled eggs on the stove top, and they want to put bread in the toaster. Since there is only one stove top and only one toaster, the cooks can not use them at the same time, but it would be fine for one cook to use the stove top while the other one uses the toaster.

These are different lock instances:

```python
stove_lock = threading.Lock()
toaster_lock = threading.Lock()

with stove_lock:
    scramble_eggs()

with toaster_lock:
    toast_bread()
```

When you call `with toaster_lock:`, if another thread is currently executing code within a separate `with toaster_lock:`, then your thread will block until the other thread calls `toaster_lock.__exit__`. However, it does not matter if another thread is currently using `stove_lock`.

But when you just do `with threading.Lock():` then you are creating a _different_ lock instance every time, and it is not actually possible for anything else to be checking against the same lock. The other threads are just blindly using their own, distinct locks and not checking in with yours.
